title: Class 1: Introduction and Basics of Economic Analysis
coverageSummary: Cost-benefit principle, economic surplus, opportunity cost, marginal analysis, comparative advantage and absolute advantage, production possibilities curves, gains from specialization and trade, supply and demand curves, market equilibrium, causes of shifts in supply and demand, market surplus, linear supply/demand equations, and causes of market failure.
reliabilityNotes: All slide text and speaker notes extracted cleanly via python-pptx; no unreadable sections. Some slides are diagram-only (axis-labeled supply/demand graphs without numeric data) and were not used as a basis for any question.

title: Class 2: Measuring the Macroeconomy
coverageSummary: GDP measurement (market value, final vs. intermediate goods, value added, expenditure/income approaches), difficulties measuring GDP, real vs. nominal GDP, CPI construction and example, price indices, inflation rate calculation, real vs. nominal interest rates, the Fisher effect, indexing, and CPI substitution/quality-adjustment bias.
reliabilityNotes: All slide text and speaker notes extracted cleanly. Several slides referencing external charts (e.g., FRED/BLS series, hyperinflation rankings) contain no extractable numeric data and were not used as a basis for any question.

title: Class 3: Labor Markets, Productivity, and Inequality
coverageSummary: Labor demand and value of marginal product, diminishing returns to labor, labor supply and reservation wage, shifts in labor demand/supply, the productivity-wage slowdown since 1970 and divergence in the 1990s, globalization and wage inequality, skill-biased technological change, the economics of superstars, policy responses to inequality, and the GDP-per-capita decomposition (Y/POP = (Y/N) x (N/POP)).
reliabilityNotes: All slide text and speaker notes (including the detailed textiles/software trade note) extracted cleanly. Chart-only slides with no data table (e.g., labor force participation chart, Piketty inequality chart) were not used as a basis for any question.

title: Class 4: Economic Growth
coverageSummary: Compound growth formula, the Rule of 72, historical living standards and cross-country growth divergence, the eight determinants of average labor productivity, diminishing returns to capital, technology and knowledge spillovers, institutions (property rights, constraints on elites, equal opportunity), the USSR case study, government growth policies (savings/investment, human capital, R&D), poverty traps, and the costs/limits of growth.
reliabilityNotes: All slide text and speaker notes extracted cleanly via python-pptx. Photo-only slides (e.g., the Bonsack machine image, China air-quality photos) contain no extractable data and were not used as a basis for any question.

title: Discussion Session 1 Solutions
coverageSummary: Worked solutions covering PPCs and gains from trade (Canada/USA honey and maple syrup), GDP calculation via expenditure/value-added/income methods (Microsoft chip example), CPI/inflation/deflating/indexing (grad student budget example), market equilibrium algebra, demand shifters (olive oil, butter/white bread, rye/white bread), and marginal analysis (lemonade stand).
reliabilityNotes: Extracted with pdftotext -layout; nearly all text is clean and complete. One exception: Section III parts (d) Deflating and (e) Indexing state the method in words but their final worked numbers did not extract as selectable text (likely rendered as an image/graphic in the original slide-style PDF). This did not block any question, since the deflating/indexing formulas are independently and fully documented in Class 2 slides 31-32. Questions in this bank use new numbers/scenarios analogous to these worked examples rather than reusing the exact figures, per the conservative-generation and no-verbatim-answer-key rules.

title: Discussion Session 2 Solutions
coverageSummary: Worked solutions covering labor demand and value of marginal product, shifts in labor demand/supply (opticians and laser eye surgery; pilots and mandatory retirement age), average labor productivity and the GDP-per-capita decomposition, and cross-country growth-rate catch-up calculations (Richland/Poorland).
reliabilityNotes: Extracted with pdftotext -layout. A few characters in the source PDF's ligatures (e.g., 'ti' in words like 'raonal' or 'producvity') rendered as stray 'ti'/'ft' fragments during extraction; these are OCR/font-encoding artifacts in the original PDF, not ambiguity in the underlying economics content, and did not affect any figures used. Questions use new numbers analogous to these worked examples rather than the exact source figures.

title: Problem Set 1 Suggested Solutions
coverageSummary: Worked solutions covering marginal analysis (compost/tomatoes, phone calling plans), PPCs and comparative/absolute advantage (Helen's dresses/bread; Tom and Susan's coffee/nuts), demand shifters and supply-and-demand shifts (vacations, pepperoni pizza, MP3s/CDs, tofu), GDP components and edge cases (inventory changes, existing-asset sales, transfer payments, multi-year international goods), CPI/inflation calculation, and real interest rates/the Fisher effect.
reliabilityNotes: Extracted with pdftotext -layout; text and tables are clean and complete. Questions in this bank use new numbers/scenarios analogous to these worked examples rather than reusing the exact figures, per the conservative-generation and no-verbatim-answer-key rules.

title: Class 5: Savings, Investment, Public Savings, and Capital Flows
coverageSummary: Saving vs. wealth, the saving rate, assets/liabilities/balance sheets/net worth, the three household saving motives (life-cycle, precautionary, bequest), real vs. nominal interest rates, national/private/public saving identities, government budget surplus/deficit and Ricardian equivalence, investment and capital formation, the value of the marginal product of capital and user cost of capital, the loanable funds market and crowding out, technology-driven shifts in investment demand, and open-economy capital flows (capital inflows/outflows, net capital inflows, the S + KI = I and NX + KI = 0 identities, and the link between trade deficits and capital inflows).
reliabilityNotes: All slide bullet text and speaker notes extracted cleanly via python-pptx across all 44 slides; no illegible or corrupted text. Roughly half the deck (slides 10-12, 15, 23-34, 43-44) is chart/photo-only content (FRED/BEA saving-rate charts, federal budget and tax-revenue charts, debt-to-GDP-by-country chart, trade-balance chart) with no extractable numeric data, and was not used as the basis for any question. The worked numeric example on slides 4 (individual balance sheet) and 17-18 (John's Fishing Business investment decision) informed question structure only; all calculation questions use freshly constructed numbers and scenarios rather than the slides' own figures, per the no-verbatim-answer-key rule. No question in this source's set required a needsReview flag.

title: Classes 6-7: Money, Banking, Financial Markets, and the Crisis of 2007-9
coverageSummary: The allocation of saving and financial intermediation, present value calculations, the definition and three functions of money, M1/M2 monetary aggregates, fractional-reserve banking and the money-creation (deposit) multiplier, the quantity equation (M x V = P x Y) and money/inflation in the long run, banking panics and the founding of the Fed, bonds (principal, coupon rate, term, credit risk, and the inverse bond-price/interest-rate relationship), stocks and the risk premium, the efficient market hypothesis, and an introductory term list for the 2007-9 financial crisis.
reliabilityNotes: All 28 slides' bullet text and tables extracted cleanly via python-pptx; one slide (27) has only a speaker-note web link with no body text. Slide 8 (M1/M2 balance table), slides 9 and 11 (Macroland consolidated bank balance sheets, 100% vs. fractional reserves), and slides 18-19 (the two-year-bond repricing walkthrough) extracted as clean tables/text and were used only to confirm calculation method; every calculation question uses freshly constructed numbers and scenarios, not the deck's own figures, per the no-verbatim-answer-key rule. Slides 22, 25, and 27 (a bond-ratings title-only slide, a duplicate EMH title-only slide, and a bare Shiller P/E link) have no extractable body content and were not used as a basis for any question. Slide 28 ('Important Terms in the 2007-9 Financial Crisis') lists terms (leverage, securitization, MBS, CDOs, CDS, shadow banking, counterparty/systemic risk) without accompanying definitions in this deck; only terms with a clear definition elsewhere in the deck (e.g., diversification on slide 23, financial intermediation on slide 4) were used as question content, per the conservative-generation rule. No question in this source's set required a needsReview flag.

filename: Guest Lecture Slides - Price Stability and Monetary Policy.pptx
title: Guest Lecture: Price Stability and the Monetary Policy of the ECB
coverageSummary: The functions and forms of money (legal tender, commercial bank money, M1 vs. M2/M3) and the 'loans create deposits' view of credit and money creation; the ECB's price-stability mandate and its symmetric 2% HICP target (and why 2% rather than 0%); the real costs of inflation (redistribution, cold progression, price distortion, risk premiums) and deflation (debt-deflation, bank failures, deflationary spirals); the medium-term policy orientation and second-round effects/wage-price spirals; why commercial banks need central bank reserves (cash withdrawals, minimum reserve requirements, interbank settlement); the monetary base and how reserves are injected (refinancing operations vs. asset purchases); the interbank money market and the overnight rate as the central bank's true control variable (not the monetary base); the pre-2007 interest rate corridor (marginal lending rate ceiling / deposit rate floor) and the post-2008 floor/ample-reserves system; forward guidance and quantitative easing/tightening; and the rationale, design, and financial-stability safeguards of the digital euro.
reliabilityNotes: All 43 slides' titles, bullet text, and (extensively detailed) speaker notes extracted cleanly via python-pptx. Slide 1 (a cover photo) and the three repeated 'Contents' section-divider slides (3, 8, 13, 22, 32, 38 -- table-of-contents lists only) and slide 10 (an ECB Governing Council photo) have no substantive content beyond a repeated agenda list and were not used as a basis for any question beyond confirming the deck's own section structure. This is a guest lecture specifically on the ECB's own framework; its concepts (e.g., the endogenous 'loans create deposits' view, the ECB's specific 2% HICP target, the digital euro) are grounded only in this deck and are not merged with Class 2's or Class 6's separate, more textbook-standard treatments of money and banking. No question in this source's set required a needsReview flag.

title: Discussion Session 3: The Market for Money (Questions + Solutions)
coverageSummary: The money demand curve (downward-sloping in the interest rate because of the opportunity cost of holding non-interest-bearing money), how Fed open-market operations (buying vs. selling bonds) and the required reserve ratio change the money supply, what does and does not shift the money demand curve (a change in the nominal interest rate is a movement along the curve, not a shift), and how credit-card availability, perceived stock riskiness, and an economic boom each shift money demand, affect the nominal interest rate, and require a specific Fed money-supply response to hold the rate constant.
reliabilityNotes: This source pairs two separate local files that must be read together, per the task's paired-source handling: DS3.pdf (the discussion-section question prompts) and DS3_solutions.pdf (the worked solutions for the same four question items). Both extracted cleanly via pdftotext -layout with no illegible text. Every ds3-* question was checked against both files together -- the scenario/prompt from DS3.pdf and the reasoning/correct answer from DS3_solutions.pdf -- never generated from only one of the two files. No question in this source's set required a needsReview flag.

title: Canvas Quiz 2 (Principles of Economics: Macroeconomics)
coverageSummary: A 10-question timed Canvas quiz covering the open-economy saving/investment identity and trade balance, private/public/national saving from a numeric income-and-spending scenario, how paying down debt affects a household's liabilities and wealth, the definition of life-cycle saving, the institutional factors behind the USSR's growth failure, crowding out, the two determinants of the cost of investment, how a lower domestic real interest rate affects capital inflows/outflows/net capital inflows, Ricardian equivalence, and the definition of capital inflows from a single country's point of view.
reliabilityNotes: Canvas's PDF export renders each question's per-question 'Correct answer' / 'Wrong answer' result label AFTER that question's own answer choices and immediately before the NEXT question's number -- a misleading artifact of the export layout that visually reads as though the label belongs to the following question, not the one it actually grades. Question 2's private/public/national saving data table also did not extract as text (rendered as an image in the source PDF). Per the user's direct clarification of their own quiz results (not re-derived from the scrambled labels or the missing table): overall score was 8/10, Question 2 was answered correctly, and Questions 3 and 10 were answered incorrectly (all other questions -- 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 -- were answered correctly). This interpretation is used instead of any naive top-to-bottom reading of the label positions. No quiz2-* question reproduces the original quiz's exact wording or the Question 2 table; each is a paraphrased 'Quiz 2 style' practice question built from the underlying concept, with fresh numbers/scenarios where applicable. Extra weight was placed on the two concepts the user missed: paying down debt reduces liabilities and increases wealth/net worth (Q3), and capital inflows are purchases of domestic assets by foreigners while capital outflows are purchases of foreign assets by domestic households/firms (Q10).

title: Instructor Study Outline and Practice Problems for the Ec S10b Midterm
coverageSummary: An instructor-released midterm study guide (Professor Savaser) covering the full first-half syllabus at the outline level (basic economic concepts, GDP measurement, CPI/inflation, labor productivity and inequality, economic growth, saving/investment, the loanable funds market, government budgets, open-economy capital flows, financial markets, and money/money creation), followed by 11 worked practice problems with full suggested solutions: the closed-economy saving/investment identity, a real loan-repayment calculation combining the Fisher equation with compound interest, a true/false question on housing prices and the household savings rate, a four-firm value-added GDP calculation, CPI-indexing the minimum wage across years, a labor-productivity counterfactual-growth comparison, a multi-part national/private/public saving problem that also introduces a perfectly vertical (interest-rate-insensitive) saving-supply curve, an inventory-drawdown GDP-timing question, a two-period stock-present-value calculation with a risk premium, a compound real-GDP growth-rate calculation, and an open-economy loanable-funds question about a foreign income shock's effect on U.S. capital inflows.
reliabilityNotes: The file is a legacy .doc (Word 97-2003) document; it converted cleanly to plain text via macOS's textutil with no illegible sections or formatting corruption. This is an instructor-released study guide, not a live/current exam: it is explicitly titled 'Study Outline for Ec S10b Midterm Exam' and 'Practice Problems for Ec S10b Midterm,' includes a 'Suggested Solutions to Extra Midterm Practice Problems' section addressed directly to students, and its own text explicitly frames the practice problems as illustrative ('only meant to be suggestive of what you might see on the exam'), not as the exam itself. The document's metadata lists 'Last Saved By: Tanseli Savaser,' matching the instructor named on the Class 1 slides. The topic-outline portion of the document (roughly its first half) was used only to confirm topic coverage/emphasis for this update's question batch, not mined directly into questions, since it is a bulleted list of topics rather than gradeable content and nearly every topic it names is already covered by the existing Class 1-6/DS1-3/Quiz 2 sources. Every one of the 11 worked practice problems' solutions was independently re-verified arithmetically before use (recomputed, not just read). One problem (the open-economy foreign-income-shock question) included a secondary NX claim in its own suggested solution that rests on a looser, more debatable mix of price/quantity trade effects than the problem's much cleaner capital-inflows claim; the adapted question in this bank keeps only the clean, defensible capital-inflows mechanism and omits the debatable NX claim rather than presenting it as a settled correct answer. No question in this bank reproduces this source's exact numbers, company/country names, or wording -- all use fresh scenarios per the no-verbatim-copying rule. No question required a needsReview flag.
